tossups.question: A form of blocks made of this material used for walls in many twentieth century buildings was invented by Gustave Falconnier. For a site known for a building made of this material, an architect designed a visitors’ pavilion known as “Da Monsta.” Garden Grove, California is home to a cathedral predominantly made with this material. A concrete sculpture by Donald Judd is outside a building named for this material, which was likely inspired by a building designed for (*) Edith Farnsworth. The plate form of this material colored black is on the exterior of a modernist building in New York designed for a distilling company. A New Canaan, Connecticut house named for this material was designed by its owner, Phillip Johnson. For 10 points, name this material used for the exterior of skyscrapers like the Seagram Building.
tossups.answer: plate glass [or glass blocks; or glass bricks; or Pigmented structural glass; or Carrara glass; or Vitrolite; or vitreous marble; prompt on crystal or the Crystal Cathedral]
tossups.answer_sanitized: plate glass [or glass blocks; or glass bricks; or Pigmented structural glass; or Carrara glass; or Vitrolite; or vitreous marble; prompt on crystal or the Crystal Cathedral]

For a site known for a building made of this material, an architect designed a visitors' pavilion known as "Da Monsta." Garden Grove, California is home to a cathedral predominantly made with this material. bonuses.leadin: This architect employed a zig-zag stepback design, consisting of what he termed "zogs," for the IDS Center in Minneapolis. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this American architect who collaborated with Mies van der Rohe on the Seagram Building.
Johnson borrowed from Mies's Farnsworth House for the design of this Connecticut building, his personal residence. Its exterior is almost entirely composed of a transparent medium.
Robert Schuller founded a ministry named for this Johnson structure, a religious building in Garden Grove, California, that houses one of the largest pipe organs in the world.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This architect employed a zig-zag stepback design, consisting of what he termed "zogs," for the IDS Center in Minneapolis. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Identify this American architect who collaborated with Mies van der Rohe on the Seagram Building.
Johnson borrowed from Mies's Farnsworth House for the design of this Connecticut building, his personal residence. Its exterior is almost entirely composed of a transparent medium.
Robert Schuller founded a ministry named for this Johnson structure, a religious building in Garden Grove, California, that houses one of the largest pipe organs in the world.

bonuses.leadin: This city is nicknamed “Fantasy Island” because of its modernist architecture and utopian layout, and much of its urban planning was executed by Lucio Costa. For 10 points each:
parts: Oscar Niemeyer designed many buildings for what South American capital, a planned city finished in 1960?

Sixteen hyperboloid columns characterize the exterior of this type of building in Brasilia, which was designed by Niemeyer. One of these buildings with a glass exterior was designed by Philip Johnson in Garden Grove, California.

The centerpiece of Niemeyer’s Pampulha Project was a building of this type. Dale Chihuly’s Fiori di Como is located in another building of this type, while one in Montreal incorporates the French and Quebec Pavilions from Expo ‘67.
answers: cathedrals [accept Cathedral of Brasilia; accept Crystal Cathedral; prompt on “churches”]
answers_sanitized: cathedrals [accept Cathedral of Brasilia; accept Crystal Cathedral; prompt on "churches"]

Sixteen hyperboloid columns characterize the exterior of this type of building in Brasilia, which was designed by Niemeyer. One of these buildings with a glass exterior was designed by Philip Johnson in Garden Grove, California.

The centerpiece of Niemeyer's Pampulha Project was a building of this type. Dale Chihuly's Fiori di Como is located in another building of this type, while one in Montreal incorporates the French and Quebec Pavilions from Expo '67.
